bins.name: Require ATT study in interconnection
bins.desc: The filer supports requiring ATT evaluation in large load interconnection studies, consistent with Order No. 2023 and CEBA's recommendation. It argues this step is necessary but not sufficient, and calls for broader RTO-level studies of DLR and topology optimization potential as well.
quotes: requiring ATTs to be evaluated in interconnection processes is valuable, it is not sufficient to enable large load interconnection at the fastest reasonable pace and lowest possible cost.

we agree with the CEBA's recommendation "that the Commission require ATTs—including dynamic line ratings—to be evaluated alongside traditional upgrades for large load interconnections, consistent with Order No. 2023, given the significant benefits identified for consumers and interconnection customers."

The time has come and gone for voluntary or incentivized consideration of GETs. It is well documented that use of GETs delivers cost reductions and cost avoidance to the benefit of consumers nationwide. Despite this, relatively few projects have been implemented.
accelerated ATT adoption is more likely to speed the interconnection of new large loads to constrained transmission systems rather than frustrate speed to power goals.

The fastest action that can be taken to identify potential untapped grid capacity is large-scale studies by RTOs. RTOs have sufficient data on grid constraints and electricity prices to use publicly available DLR datasets and/or software from the National Laboratory of the Rockies (NLR) to quickly determine the likely magnitude of value from DLR.

we urge the Commission to encourage all RTOs to host forums to investigate the opportunity for ATTs and other solutions to increase capacity and improve affordability and how to integrate ATTs into all aspects of an RTO's processes.

ATTs can deliver meaningful increases in transmission capacity to facilitate the interconnection of large load in less than three years. While recent deployments by PG&E, Georgia Power and others validate this potential, utility adoption remains limited.

bins.name: ATTs reduce costs for ratepayers
bins.desc: The filer argues that ATTs lower congestion costs, improve investment efficiency, and protect ratepayers. It cites Basin Electric's DLR deployment recovering capital costs in 38 days and saving over $20 million in 2024, and contends that reporting requirements on GETs potential are essential to ensuring just and reasonable rates.
quotes: requiring ATTs to be evaluated in interconnection processes is valuable, it is not sufficient to enable large load interconnection at the fastest reasonable pace and lowest possible cost.

The time has come and gone for voluntary or incentivized consideration of GETs. It is well documented that use of GETs delivers cost reductions and cost avoidance to the benefit of consumers nationwide. Despite this, relatively few projects have been implemented.

The transparency envisioned by these reporting requirements is also critical to ensuring just and reasonable rates by identifying opportunities to maximize the use of our existing grid, to the benefit of ratepayers across the country, and to ensure our investments in new transmission are efficient and cost effective—key components of just and reasonable rates.

We urge the Commission to act, through a policy statement or order directing reports, to encourage transmission owners and RTOs to develop processes focused on improving grid efficiency. These processes could unlock latent grid capacity in the timescales needed to win the AI race–the next two to three years—as well as to address the growing affordability crisis.

The WATT Coalition has proposed a federal shared savings incentive that allows transmission owners to profit based on a portion of the congestion cost savings from GETs deployments. This directly links utility profits to consumer benefits and operational efficiency.

ATTs can deliver meaningful increases in transmission capacity to facilitate the interconnection of large load in less than three years. While recent deployments by PG&E, Georgia Power and others validate this potential, utility adoption remains limited.

bins.name: Operational planning reform needed
bins.desc: The filer contends that transmission owners currently lack staff and processes dedicated to grid efficiency, and urges the Commission to direct operational planning reforms. It recommends monthly, quarterly, and yearly efficiency analyses that incorporate GETs, arguing these can unlock latent capacity within two to three years.
quotes: accelerated ATT adoption is more likely to speed the interconnection of new large loads to constrained transmission systems rather than frustrate speed to power goals.
very few, if any, transmission owners have employees in engineering or operations roles where one of their primary job responsibilities is to ensure efficient grid operation.

We urge the Commission to act, through a policy statement or order directing reports, to encourage transmission owners and RTOs to develop processes focused on improving grid efficiency. These processes could unlock latent grid capacity in the timescales needed to win the AI race–the next two to three years—as well as to address the growing affordability crisis.

bins.name: Voluntary adoption has failed
bins.desc: The filer, quoting Industrial Customer Organizations, argues that voluntary GETs consideration has produced too little adoption despite well-documented benefits. It contends the Commission must take a more aggressive posture and require GETs where cost-effective.
quotes: The time has come and gone for voluntary or incentivized consideration of GETs. It is well documented that use of GETs delivers cost reductions and cost avoidance to the benefit of consumers nationwide. Despite this, relatively few projects have been implemented.

bins.name: ATTs accelerate, not slow, interconnection
bins.desc: The filer directly rebuts the argument that ATT requirements would frustrate speed-to-power goals, arguing the opposite: ATTs on constrained systems speed up large load interconnection. Recent utility deployments demonstrate capacity gains achievable in under three years.
quotes: accelerated ATT adoption is more likely to speed the interconnection of new large loads to constrained transmission systems rather than frustrate speed to power goals.

ATTs can deliver meaningful increases in transmission capacity to facilitate the interconnection of large load in less than three years. While recent deployments by PG&E, Georgia Power and others validate this potential, utility adoption remains limited.

bins.name: RTO studies and reporting requirements
bins.desc: The filer recommends that the Commission require RTOs to study DLR and topology optimization potential using existing grid data, and require transmission owners to report investment efficiency metrics. These reporting requirements would inform future rulemakings, including the pending DLR implementation docket.
quotes: The fastest action that can be taken to identify potential untapped grid capacity is large-scale studies by RTOs. RTOs have sufficient data on grid constraints and electricity prices to use publicly available DLR datasets and/or software from the National Laboratory of the Rockies (NLR) to quickly determine the likely magnitude of value from DLR.

The transparency envisioned by these reporting requirements is also critical to ensuring just and reasonable rates by identifying opportunities to maximize the use of our existing grid, to the benefit of ratepayers across the country, and to ensure our investments in new transmission are efficient and cost effective—key components of just and reasonable rates.

bins.name: New grid efficiency processes at utilities
bins.desc: The filer proposes specific monthly, quarterly, and yearly operational planning processes for transmission owners and RTOs, including congestion intervention thresholds, DLR deployment timelines, and comprehensive three-year efficiency studies. It argues the Commission should encourage these through a policy statement or directed reports.
quotes: very few, if any, transmission owners have employees in engineering or operations roles where one of their primary job responsibilities is to ensure efficient grid operation.

We urge the Commission to act, through a policy statement or order directing reports, to encourage transmission owners and RTOs to develop processes focused on improving grid efficiency. These processes could unlock latent grid capacity in the timescales needed to win the AI race–the next two to three years—as well as to address the growing affordability crisis.

bins.name: Commission's ATT record is outdated
bins.desc: The filer notes that the Commission's record on ATT deployments and barriers dates largely from before 2024, and recommends directing reports and inviting comments from transmission owners and RTOs to update the record. The filer provides a list of specific questions to structure that information gathering.
quotes: The Commission's record on ATT deployments and obstacles to deployments is several years old. The record in Docket No. RM-24-6-000 on the Implementation of Dynamic Line Ratings is largely from before 2024.

we suggest that the Commission direct reports and invite further comments in an open or new docketed proceeding, focusing in particular on transmission owners and RTOs to refresh the Commission's record around the state of ATT adoption.

bins.name: ATT-specific incentive guidance needed
bins.desc: The filer urges FERC to issue a policy statement clarifying what incentives for ATT projects are permissible under Order No. 679. It identifies shared savings incentives and ROE adders as candidate structures and notes that no ATT projects have sought incentive treatment despite over $80 billion in total incentive applications.
quotes: we encourage FERC to issue a policy statement with further guidance on what kinds of incentives for ATTs projects might be just and reasonable under the existing requirements of Order No. 679.

The WATT Coalition has proposed a federal shared savings incentive that allows transmission owners to profit based on a portion of the congestion cost savings from GETs deployments. This directly links utility profits to consumer benefits and operational efficiency.

bins.name: RTO-hosted forums for knowledge sharing
bins.desc: The filer urges the Commission to encourage RTOs to host regional forums on ATT integration and to convene a FERC technical conference in 2027 to hear from all regions. The goal is to accelerate cross-utility learning and best practice adoption.
quotes: we urge the Commission to encourage all RTOs to host forums to investigate the opportunity for ATTs and other solutions to increase capacity and improve affordability and how to integrate ATTs into all aspects of an RTO's processes.

bins.name: PJM region ATT adoption gaps
bins.desc: The filer notes that only Dominion Energy among PJM transmission owners has significant DLR adoption planned, and that PJM Transmission Owners oppose ATT requirements. It argues Commission action is needed to overcome regional resistance.
quotes: we urge the Commission to encourage all RTOs to host forums to investigate the opportunity for ATTs and other solutions to increase capacity and improve affordability and how to integrate ATTs into all aspects of an RTO's processes.

bins.name: MISO region GETs opportunity
bins.desc: The filer cites MISO data showing that ambient and emergency ratings could have saved $168 million in real-time congestion costs in Winter 2023, and notes MISO has taken incremental steps including a congestion reconfiguration process.
quotes: we urge the Commission to encourage all RTOs to host forums to investigate the opportunity for ATTs and other solutions to increase capacity and improve affordability and how to integrate ATTs into all aspects of an RTO's processes.

bins.name: SPP topology optimization leadership
bins.desc: The filer highlights SPP's tariff filing to implement economic topology optimization as a leading example of constructive RTO policy, citing studies showing potential congestion savings of $18 to $44 million annually in SPP's footprint.
quotes: we urge the Commission to encourage all RTOs to host forums to investigate the opportunity for ATTs and other solutions to increase capacity and improve affordability and how to integrate ATTs into all aspects of an RTO's processes.

bins.name: ISO-NE regional forum inclusion
bins.desc: The filer recommends that all RTOs, including ISO-NE, host forums on ATT integration opportunities as part of a Commission-encouraged regional knowledge-sharing initiative.
quotes: we urge the Commission to encourage all RTOs to host forums to investigate the opportunity for ATTs and other solutions to increase capacity and improve affordability and how to integrate ATTs into all aspects of an RTO's processes.
